accomplishments: Create a button that responds when you click it
Write your first line of JavaScript code

do.title: Make a Button Say Hello
steps: Open VS Code (or go to CodePen.io in your browser)
Create a new file and save it as talking-button.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save the file
Double-click the file to open it in your browser
Click the button — you'll see a popup appear!
do.code: My First JavaScript
do.result: You just created an interactive button that responds to clicks using JavaScript!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the popup message "Hello! You clicked me!" after clicking your button.

concepts.name: The <button> tag + onclick
concepts.definition: The button creates a clickable element. The onclick attribute tells it what JavaScript to run when clicked.
concepts.analogy: A doorbell button on a house. onclick is the wiring that connects it to the chime.

concepts.name: alert() function
concepts.definition: JavaScript code that shows a popup message. Every browser understands alert(). Great for testing, not great for real websites (popups are annoying).
concepts.analogy: A sticky note that pops up in front of everything until you press OK.

concepts.name: HTML creates elements, JavaScript makes them DO things
concepts.definition: HTML builds the structure (the button). JavaScript adds behavior (what happens when you click). They work together but are different languages.
concepts.analogy: HTML is building a car. JavaScript is the engine that makes it drive.

understand.keyTakeaway: HTML creates elements. JavaScript makes them DO things. onclick + alert() is the simplest way to start.

build.goal: Make the button show a personalized greeting with YOUR name

codeComparison.new:
<button onclick="alert('Hello, Sarah! Welcome to JavaScript!')">Click Me!</button>

<!-- Replace "Sarah" with YOUR actual name! -->

build.hint: Make sure to keep the quotes around your text: alert('like this'), not alert(like this). The quotes tell JavaScript this is text to display.

challenge.title: Create a Compliment Button
challenge.mission: Create a button that gives you a different compliment each time you click it, picked randomly from 3 options.
requirements: Button text says "Give Me a Compliment"
When clicked, shows one of 3 different compliments
Each compliment is encouraging and positive
challenge.starterCode: Daily Compliment Generator
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add 5 compliments instead of 3! Change the random number generator to pick from 1-5.

capabilities: Create interactive buttons with JavaScript
Use alert() to show messages
Use onclick to make elements respond to clicks
Write basic JavaScript functions
Generate random numbers with Math.random()
Use if/else statements to make decisions

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn how to change what's on the page WITHOUT annoying popups. You'll make text appear, disappear, and change — all with JavaScript.

accomplishments: Make text on your page change when you click a button
Create a name tag that updates in real-time
Change multiple things on the page at once

do.title: Make a Headline Change
steps: Open VS Code or CodePen
Create a new file and save it as changing-text.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in browser
Click the button — watch the headline change instantly!
do.code: Hello World
do.result: The headline text changed WITHOUT reloading the page. That's JavaScript manipulating the DOM!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the headline AFTER clicking (should say "I just changed this with JavaScript!")

concepts.name: The id attribute
concepts.definition: Gives an HTML element a unique name (like a name tag). Every id on a page must be unique. In JavaScript, you find it with #id.
concepts.analogy: A student ID number. No two students have the same one.

concepts.name: document.querySelector()
concepts.definition: JavaScript's way of saying "go find this element on the page." Use #id for IDs, .class for classes. Returns the first matching element.
concepts.analogy: A search function — you describe what you're looking for, it finds it and hands it to you.

concepts.name: .textContent
concepts.definition: Changes the text inside an element. Whatever you put after = becomes the new text. Doesn't change HTML structure, just the visible text.
concepts.analogy: Erasing what's written on a whiteboard and writing something new.

concepts.name: The = sign in JavaScript
concepts.definition: In JavaScript, = means "assign this value" (not "equals" like math). x = 5 means "make x equal 5." To check equality, use === instead.
concepts.analogy: Putting something INTO a box (=) vs. checking what's IN the box (===).

understand.keyTakeaway: Select an element with querySelector(), then change it with .textContent. That's the core pattern for DOM manipulation.

build.goal: Create a name tag that updates with YOUR name
build.hint: Keep the quotes around your name: 'Sarah' not Sarah. Without quotes, JavaScript thinks you're talking about a variable.

challenge.title: Build a Mood Ring
challenge.mission: Create three buttons that change a text display to show different moods with emojis.
requirements: One text area that displays the current mood
Each button changes the text + emoji
The mood text should be big and centered
challenge.starterCode:
How are you feeling?

Click a button to set your mood
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add two more mood buttons: "Excited" and "Calm" with their own emojis!

capabilities: Change text on a page without reloading
Use querySelector() to find elements
Use .textContent to update what elements say
Give elements IDs and select them with #
Create multiple functions that change the same element
Build interactive interfaces that respond to user actions

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn how to change not just the TEXT, but the STYLE — colors, sizes, backgrounds. You'll build a theme switcher that transforms your entire page.

accomplishments: Change the background color of your page with a button click
Build a light/dark mode toggle switch
Control any CSS property with JavaScript

do.title: Build a Color-Changing Background
steps: Open VS Code or CodePen
Create a new file: color-changer.html
Copy the code below
Save and open in your browser
Click each button — watch the background change instantly!
do.code: Click to Change Colors!
do.result: JavaScript changed the CSS of your page in real-time. No page reload needed!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the page with one of the colored backgrounds.

concepts.name: document.body
concepts.definition: Selects the <body> element of your HTML page. It's the main container holding everything visible.
concepts.analogy: The walls and floor of a room. When you paint them, the whole room looks different.

concepts.name: .style property
concepts.definition: The bridge between JavaScript and CSS. Lets you read or change any CSS property on any element.
concepts.analogy: A remote control for your page's appearance.

concepts.name: camelCase for CSS properties
concepts.definition: CSS uses hyphens (background-color), JavaScript uses camelCase (backgroundColor). font-size becomes fontSize, border-radius becomes borderRadius.
concepts.analogy: Two languages for the same thing — like "red" in English and "rojo" in Spanish.

concepts.name: Changing multiple styles at once
concepts.definition: You can set multiple .style properties in one function to completely transform an element's appearance.
concepts.analogy: Redecorating a room — change the paint, furniture, and lighting all at once.

concepts.name: if/else for toggling
concepts.definition: Check the current state, then switch to the opposite. if dark mode is on, switch to light. else switch to dark.
concepts.analogy: A light switch — check if it's on, then flip it.

understand.keyTakeaway: If you can style it with CSS, you can change it with JavaScript using element.style.propertyName.

build.goal: Build a dark mode toggle that switches between light and dark
build.hint: The if/else checks which mode you're in, then switches to the opposite.

challenge.title: Build a Theme Picker
challenge.mission: Create a page with 4 theme buttons (Ocean, Forest, Sunset, Midnight) that each change background, text, and heading colors.
requirements: Each theme changes background, text, AND heading color
Large heading that says "Choose Your Theme"
challenge.starterCode: Choose Your Theme
challenge.bonusChallenge: Make the buttons themselves change color to match the theme they activate!

capabilities: Change any CSS property with JavaScript
Use .style.backgroundColor, .style.color, .style.fontSize
Convert CSS property names to JavaScript camelCase
Build dark mode toggles
Create complete theme switchers

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about VARIABLES — how to store information, remember user choices, and make your code smarter.

accomplishments: Store a user's name and display it on the page
Build a click counter that remembers how many times you clicked
Create a personalized greeting that updates dynamically

do.title: Create a Click Counter
steps: Open VS Code or CodePen
Create click-counter.html
Copy the code below
Save and open in browser
Click the button multiple times — watch the number increase!
do.code:
Click Counter

0
do.result: The variable clickCount REMEMBERS how many times you clicked!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the counter at 10 or higher.

concepts.name: What is a variable?
concepts.definition: A named container that holds information. let clickCount = 0 creates a box labeled "clickCount" with 0 inside.
concepts.analogy: A labeled box. The label is the name, what's inside is the value.

concepts.name: let vs const
concepts.definition: let = value CAN change later (counters, scores). const = value CANNOT change. Use const by default, let when you know it'll change.
concepts.analogy: let = a whiteboard (erase and rewrite). const = a carved stone tablet (permanent).

concepts.name: Variable scope
concepts.definition: Variables OUTSIDE functions persist between calls. Variables INSIDE functions are created and destroyed each time.
concepts.analogy: Outside = whiteboard on the wall (stays). Inside = sticky note (thrown away after use).

concepts.name: .value for input fields
concepts.definition: Gets whatever the user typed into an <input> element. document.getElementById("name-input").value returns their text.
concepts.analogy: Reading what someone wrote on a form — .value is the answer they filled in.

concepts.name: Template literals
concepts.definition: Backticks (`) let you insert variables into text using ${variable}. Example: `Hello, ${name}!` becomes "Hello, Sarah!"
concepts.analogy: Mad Libs — the ${} are the blanks that get filled in with your variables.

concepts.name: The ++ shorthand
concepts.definition: clickCount++ is shorthand for clickCount = clickCount + 1. Both add 1 to the variable.
concepts.analogy: A tally counter — click once, number goes up by one.

understand.keyTakeaway: Variables let your program REMEMBER things. Without variables, your code has no memory.

build.goal: Build a personalized greeter using .value and template literals
build.hint: Template literals use BACKTICKS (`), not regular quotes. The backtick key is usually above Tab.

challenge.title: Build a Score Tracker
challenge.mission: Create a game score tracker with +1, +5, and Reset buttons. Track current score AND highest score ever.
requirements: Reset sets current to 0 but keeps high score
High score updates when current beats it
challenge.starterCode:
🎮 Score Tracker

Current Score
0
🏆 Highest Score
0
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a +10 button and make the high score turn GOLD when beaten!

capabilities: Create variables with let and const
Store numbers, text, and any data
Update variables as your program runs
Get user input with .value
Use template literals to insert variables into text
Build counters, trackers, and dynamic greetings

bridge.preview: Module 1 complete! You've built buttons, text changers, theme switchers, and score trackers. In Module 2, you'll work with lists, make decisions with logic, and build real interactive apps.
